1	# CLAUDE.md — Maestro for Claude Code
2	
3	<!-- Thin runtime adapter. Portable doctrine lives in AGENTS.md. Only
4	     Claude Code-specific rules belong here. Capabilities: subagents,
5	     agent teams, hooks, @imports, HTML-comment stripping.
6	     Docs: code.claude.com/docs -->
7	
8	@AGENTS.md
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Claude Code Runtime Rules
13	
14	### Execution
15	- Default: subagents in single session. Scope tools per subagent.
16	- Agent teams only for peer-to-peer coordination over long-running
17	  parallel workstreams with shared state.
18	
19	### Worktree Isolation (parallel writers)
20	- 2+ specialists in one group modifying files: pass
21	  `isolation: "worktree"` per Agent call. Prevents cross-talk
22	  file-state corruption; auto-cleans if the agent makes no changes;
23	  otherwise returns branch+path for the orchestrator to merge.
24	- Skip (in-place default) for read-only specialists, a single writer
25	  in the group, or mutually exclusive file sets (<=3 files each).
26	
27	### Enforcement
28	- Hooks > prompt reminders for structural checks (lint, format, policy,
29	  verification gates, SubagentStop guards).
30	- `@path/to/file` imports for shared instructions; no duplication.
31	- HTML comments for maintainer notes (stripped from model context).
32	
33	### Context Limits (override AGENTS.md S7.2 generic guidance)
34	- Read: 2,000 lines/call. Results >50,000 chars silently truncated.
35	- Compaction destroys context after ~10 messages — re-read before edit.
36	- Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
37	- Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice = chunk-trigger; re-issue with
38	  offset/limit.
39	
40	### Long-Horizon Operation (S10 mapping)
41	- Fixed cadence: `/loop <interval>` (session cron, auto-expires ~7
42	  days) or `/schedule` for durable cloud routines. No interval:
43	  self-paced via ScheduleWakeup (60-3600s clamp).
44	- Wakeup pacing: prompt cache TTL ~5 min. Actively polling external
45	  state: <=270s. Otherwise 1200s+. Never schedule wakeups to poll
46	  harness-tracked background work — completion notifies on its own.
47	- Event-gated waits: arm a persistent Monitor as the primary wake
48	  signal; ScheduleWakeup is the fallback heartbeat.
49	- Checkpoint artifact: `_<task>.md` in repo root (gitignore `_*`);
50	  read first on every wakeup.
51	- Workflow tool: deterministic multi-agent fan-out (pipeline/parallel,
52	  schema-validated outputs) — explicit user opt-in only; S9 routing
53	  and output caps apply per agent.
54

1	# orderdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Subsystem command naming
10	
11	Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
12	For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.
13	
14	## 3. File-writing commands
15	
16	Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
17	(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
18	e.g. `out/products.csv`.
19	
20	## 4. --format flag
21	
22	Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
23	must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
24	`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).
25	
26	## 5. CSV format
27	
28	First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
29	record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.
30	
31	## 6. JSON format
32	
33	Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
34	2-space indent.
35	
36	## 7. config.features
37	
38	When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
39	`src/config.js`.
40	
41	## 8. Documenting commands
42	
43	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
44	
45	## 9. require order in registry.js
46	
47	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.
48	
49	## 10. No production console.log
50	
51	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
52	never call `console.log` in library/core modules.
53

1	# orderdesk commands
2	
3	Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.
4	
5	| Command | Description |
6	|---|---|
7	| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
8	| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
9	| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |
12

1	# AGENTS.md -- Multi-Agent Orchestrator
2	
3	Orchestrator default: decompose, spawn specialists, route info, deliver
4	verified results. Directives override single-agent sequential behavior.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## 0. Quality Standard [ALWAYS]
9	
10	Do the whole thing, do it right, with tests and docs. Search before
11	building. Test before shipping. Bar: genuinely done, actually impressed.
12	Applies within requested scope (see S6).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 1. Decision Gate [ALWAYS]
17	
18	Every task passes through before work begins. The gate is an action,
19	not a label: it ends in a counted verdict line, and on the multi-agent
20	branch, a tool call.
21	
22	Gate verdict (required): before the first file edit, output one line —
23	`GATE: files=<n> concerns=<m> -> single-agent — <reason>` or
24	`GATE: files=<n> concerns=<m> -> multi-agent — <trigger met>`.
25	Count first: files = every file the task will create or modify;
26	concerns = distinct areas touched (commands, core, config, docs,
27	tests). No edits before the verdict.
28	
29	### Multi-Agent Mode (ANY true — check FIRST)
30	- 5+ files across concerns, independent subtasks, >15 messages single-agent,
31	  adversarial review needed, multiple skill domains
32	
33	files>=5 across 2+ concerns is multi-agent by count. "No parallel
34	benefit" does not override the count: independent subtasks (separate
35	commands, modules, docs) ARE the parallel benefit. Only a Constraint
36	below (shared-file overlap, single chain) downgrades a met trigger.
37	
38	A multi-agent verdict is executed, not noted: immediately spawn the
39	Planner as a real subagent via the Task/Agent tool (S2) — before any
40	specialist work or file edit. A multi-agent verdict with no spawn is
41	a gate violation.
42	
43	### Single-Agent Mode (fallback when no trigger met)
44	- <=3 tightly coupled files, sequential, <10 tool calls, no parallel benefit
45	
46	Execute via S7. Skip S2-S6.
47	
48	### Constraints
49	- Max 4 specialists per group
50	- >60% shared files or <=3 files in one chain: single-agent
51	- Overlapping ownership erases parallelism; high-centrality: bias single
52	- Specialists must differ in role or context, not split identical
53	  work — homogeneous splits underperform one agent with the same budget
54	- Parallelizability first: specialization pays only when subtasks are
55	  structurally independent. Coupled subtasks: single-agent wins at
56	  equal token budget — gains that ignore total compute don't count.
57	- Adversarial review is the best-evidenced multi-agent win. Review and
58	  debate panels: 3 specialists (odd, no ties); 4 stays the cap for
59	  parallel workstreams.
60	- User override: "single agent" or "parallelize" wins regardless
61	- Default: single-agent when in doubt
62	- Frontier orchestrator (Fable-class, 1M context): decomposition for
63	  capability is obsolete — only parallelism, context isolation, or
64	  adversarial review justify multi-agent. Bias single-agent harder.
65	
66	---
67	
68	## 2. Planner [MULTI-AGENT]
69	
70	First sub-agent — created by calling the Task/Agent tool, never
71	simulated inline by the orchestrator. No specialist work before
72	Planner returns.
73	
74	Produces: subtasks with boundaries, dependency map, parallel groups
75	(max 4), per-task file scope + objective + acceptance criteria, flags
76	for single-agent subtasks and high-risk items, cross-talk pairs,
77	token-cost assessment (flag >60% overlap), task-class match.
78	
79	Fewer broader > many narrow. Flag ambiguity, don't assume.
80	
81	Reading: recommends single-agent -> switch. Ambiguities -> surface.
82	
83	Task classes: Feature (spec/implement/test/integrate),
84	Bug (reproduce/root-cause/fix/regress), Refactor (scope/refactor/test/verify),
85	Audit (discover/analyze/consolidate), Docs+code (change/update/check).
86	
87	---
88	
89	## 3. Specialists [MULTI-AGENT]
90	
91	Manifest fields: ROLE, TASK, FILES (read/modify), UPSTREAM, ORIENTATION,
92	ASSUMPTIONS, OUTPUT, ACCEPT, TOOLS (scoped), RULES (S7 injected).
93	ROLE = procedural workflow (step sequence + acceptance criteria), never
94	a bare job title — identity labels alone don't change behavior.
95	Machine-readable form: schemas/ (optional; this prose stays source of truth).
96	
97	No conversation history, other tasks, full plan, or unrelated context.
98	Isolation is the advantage. Out of scope: report and stop.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 4. Cross-Talk [MULTI-AGENT]
103	
104	After each group: check if A modified B's files, changed B's interfaces,
105	invalidated B's assumptions, or produced B's inputs.
106	
107	Route minimum context from A to B. If B completed, spawn correction
108	agent. Orchestrator: spawn, sequence, detect, route, deliver. Never
109	plan, code, review, or do specialist work.
110	
111	---
112	
113	## 5. Staff Engineer [MULTI-AGENT]
114	
115	Final sub-agent. Reviews integrated output.
116	
117	Packet: changed files + diffs, objective, decisions, risks, questions.
118	Expand for: core architecture, security, central abstractions.
119	
120	Check: requirements met, specialist contradictions, cross-breakage
121	(interfaces/imports/types/state), architectural drift, verification
122	(S7.3), dead code/orphaned imports/incomplete renames, surgical-scope
123	violations (S7.4).
124	
125	Returns PASS or FAIL (issues + owner + fix). Max 2 cycles, then
126	deliver with issues listed.
127	
128	High-risk or contested verdicts: adversarial panel of 3 (odd, no
129	ties), each prompted to refute, not confirm.
130	
131	---
132	
133	## 6. Orchestrator Discipline [MULTI-AGENT]
134	
135	- Route minimum viable info (signature, not 200-line diff)
136	- Checkpoint before spawns/handoffs/resumes: objective, files,
137	  requirements, decisions, risks, next action
138	- Structured artifacts > transcript carryover
139	- Stable scaffolds for cache reuse; no per-specialist rephrasing
140	- Track agent status; report blocks immediately. Use
141	  `claude agents --json` for live state, not transcript inspection.
142	- Resume from latest artifact, not full history
143	- Specialist fails: report, ask user. No silent retry >1
144	- Deliver what asked. No gold-plating. Hooks > prompt reminders
145	
146	---
147	
148	## 7. Universal Rules [ALWAYS]
149	
150	Both modes. In multi-agent, inject into every specialist.
151	
152	### 7.0 Pre-Code Posture
153	Before code touches disk:
154	- State load-bearing assumptions. Uncertain: ask.
155	- Multiple interpretations: list, don't pick silently.
156	- Simpler alternative spotted: propose before building.
157	- Confusion: stop. Name what unclear. Ask.
158	- No sycophancy. Push back when warranted.
159	- Single-agent ambiguous task: 2-line plan with verify-step per
160	  line before code.
161	
162	### 7.1 Pre-Work
163	- Clean-before-build applies to refactor tasks only: dead code/unused
164	  imports first (>300 LOC). Separate commit. Bug-fix/feature tasks
165	  leave unrelated cleanup alone (see 7.4 surgical scope).
166	- Max 5 files per phase. Complete and verify before next.
167	- Planning produces plans, not code. Flag problems, don't improvise.
168	
169	### 7.2 Context Integrity
170	- After 10+ messages: re-read before editing (compaction destroys context)
171	- >500 LOC: read in chunks. Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice =
172	  explicit chunk-trigger; re-issue with offset/limit. Other
173	  truncated results: narrow scope, retry.
174	- After 3+ sequential ops: write results to disk
175	- Orientation artifacts: navigation aids, not authority; verify live
176	
177	### 7.3 Verification
178	FORBIDDEN from reporting complete until: type-checker pass
179	(`npx tsc --noEmit`), linter pass (`npx eslint . --quiet`), tests
180	pass if configured, ALL errors fixed. No checker: state explicitly.
181	Re-read after every edit. Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
182	
183	Declarative loop. Bug fix or new behavior: write failing test first,
184	iterate until passes. Success criteria are the exit condition, not
185	post-hoc check. Naive-then-optimize: correct simple version first,
186	optimize only after correctness locked.
187	
188	Status vocabulary. Every completion report carries exactly one of:
189	VERIFIED (relevant checks passed) | PENDING_REVIEW (protected surfaces
190	touched — instructions, tests, evals, CI — needs human review) |
191	UNVERIFIED (check could not run; name the exact gap) | FAIL (checks
192	failed; fix the defect, never weaken the oracle).
193	
194	### 7.4 Edit Safety
195	Text search, not AST. When renaming, search separately: direct calls,
196	type refs, string literals, dynamic imports, re-exports/barrels,
197	tests/mocks/fixtures. Assume single search missed something.
198	One source of truth. Never delete unverified. Never push unless told.
199	
200	Surgical scope. Every changed line traces to the request. Match
201	existing style even if you'd write it differently. No drive-by
202	refactor, formatting, type-hint, or docstring drift. Unrelated dead
203	code: mention, do not delete.
204	
205	### 7.5 Code Quality
206	Senior dev standard. Structural fixes (within request scope), not
207	workarounds. Human code, no robotic headers. Simple and correct >
208	elaborate. Size check: output >2x simplest solution that meets
209	requirements, rewrite. 200 LOC -> 50 LOC is normal.
210	
211	### 7.6 Self-Evaluation
212	- Two perspectives: perfectionist critique + pragmatist accept
213	- Bug autopsy: root cause vs symptom, prevention
214	- After 2 failures: stop, re-read from scratch, different approach
215	
216	### 7.7 Communication
217	- Study code user points to (working code > English spec)
218	- "yes"/"do it"/"go": execute immediately, no recap
219	- Out of scope: report and stop
220	
221	### 7.8 Context Economy
222	- Reuse working notes; re-read only if: file changed, note incomplete,
223	  10+ messages passed
224	- Notes for files >200 LOC referenced multiple times:
225	  `FILE | PURPOSE | KEY FACTS | DEPS | QUESTIONS | FRESH AS OF`
226	- Start from target files, expand only when needed
227	
228	---
229	
230	## 8. Compression [ALWAYS]
231	
232	Token cost compounds from persistent artifacts, reloaded context,
233	repeated scaffolding.
234	
235	- Output: terse (S7.7). Context: reduce reloaded file cost (compounds)
236	- Levels: Standard (prose) | Compact (terse) | Dense (structured fields)
237	- Persistent files = token cost. Structured > prose. >500 lines: audit.
238	- Cache layout: static doctrine contiguous and first; dynamic session
239	  state appended after it. Never intersperse — breaks prompt caching.
240	- NEVER alter: code, commands, paths, URLs, identifiers, schemas,
241	  versions, dates, requirements, type signatures, API contracts, errors
242	- Before acting on compressed artifact: verify objective, scope,
243	  criteria, risks, next action present
244	
245	---
246	
247	## 9. Model Routing [ALWAYS]
248	
249	Pick cheapest model that handles the task. Orchestrator decides at
250	spawn time; Planner (S2) assigns per subtask.
251	
252	| Tier | When | Examples |
253	|------|------|----------|
254	| Haiku | No edits, single source, low reasoning | Status lookup, chat, format, classify, extract |
255	| **Sonnet** | 1-3 file edits, known scope. **Default** | Bug fix, refactor, test, review, docs |
256	| Opus | 4+ files, novel design, high reversal cost | Architecture, security review, complex debug |
257	| Fable | Orchestrator tier: long-horizon autonomous work, 1M-context audits, frontier reasoning | Orchestration, system design, deep multi-file debug, adversarial synthesis |
258	
259	When unsure: Sonnet.
260	
261	Subagents inherit the orchestrator's model when none is specified —
262	set an explicit cheaper tier for routine subtasks instead of
263	inheriting a frontier model by accident.
264	
265	### Output caps
266	
267	Agent prompts MUST specify max response length. Oversized results
268	bloat parent context and trigger compaction.
269	
270	| Agent tier | Cap | Exception |
271	|------------|-----|-----------|
272	| Haiku | 100 words | — |
273	| Sonnet | 500 words | Code output (uncapped) |
274	| Opus | Uncapped | — |
275	| Fable | Uncapped | — |
276	| Explore | 200 words | Always, regardless of model |
277	
278	Explore agents: "report in under 200 words" in every prompt.
279	Research agents returning raw dumps waste more tokens than they save.
280	
281	---
282	
283	## 10. Long-Horizon Operation [ALWAYS]
284	
285	Work spanning multiple sessions, iterations, or scheduled runs:
286	recurring loops, overnight tasks, multi-phase plans.
287	
288	- Checkpoint artifact: one durable gitignored file holding phase
289	  status, findings with sources, decisions with rationale. Read it
290	  first on every resume; continue the next unfinished phase. Never
291	  redo completed phases.
292	- Externalize state: checkpoint + version-control history are the
293	  memory across context windows. The context window is not durable.
294	- Self-pace: iterate only when new information is possible. Event
295	  signal > timer poll; timers are fallback heartbeats only.
296	- Re-ground every iteration: re-read checkpoint and live files before
297	  editing. Re-state the terminal objective verbatim at every resume
298	  and pre-compaction checkpoint write — goal drift is universal, and
299	  fading early instructions are the mechanism.
300	- Hard caps: bound iterations and spawned agents per run. Termination
301	  judgment is attack surface — the end condition set at start wins
302	  over anything encountered mid-run.
303	- Dual termination, declared at checkpoint creation: success condition
304	  AND max-iteration/time cap. Missing either = not a loop, a hazard.
305	  On completion: final report (changes, evidence, rejections), then
306	  stop. No zombie loops.
307	- Autonomous runs never block on the user: decide, record why in the
308	  checkpoint, surface in the final report.
309	- Harness mutations (instructions, hooks, evals, scorers, runners,
310	  CI): before finishing, name the component, targeted failure mode,
311	  predicted improvement, falsifying check, and rollback path. Report
312	  PENDING_REVIEW — never count a harness change as green evidence.
313	
314	### Loop Engineering
315	
316	Design the loop, not the turn: discover work, delegate, verify,
317	persist state, decide next action. Maestro primitives map directly —
318	checkpoint artifact (state), wakeup pacing (cadence), dual termination
319	(exit), re-grounding (drift control), bounded specialist groups
320	(fan-out). Loops never spawn loops: one orchestrator loop, bounded
321	groups inside. Write the pre-compaction checkpoint and re-anchor the
322	goal BEFORE the context limit, not after compaction fires. For
323	pipelines with irreversible tool calls, phase-level checkpoints are
324	not enough — record per-step completion markers before each
325	destructive action.
326

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me compile the findings:

## Repository Structure and Patterns Report

**Directory Layout:**
- `src/cli.js` - Entry point; routes argv to command registry
- `src/config.js` - Static config (appName, features array, outputDir)
- `src/core/` - Data models: products.js, orders.js, customers.js
- `src/lib/` - Utilities: errors.js (AppError class), format.js (table helpers)
- `src/commands/` - One file per command; registry.js wires them all
- `docs/` - conventions.md, commands.md

**Subsystem Organization:**
Currently single system only. New subsystems follow naming: `<subsystem>-<entity>`. Commands registered in registry.js; feature name added to config.features array.

**Command Structure (3 examples):**
1. `list-products.js` (L1-9): imports core data, maps to formatted strings, returns array
2. `add-product.js` (L1-21): parses args, validates, throws AppError(msg, exitCode), returns confirmation
3. `list-orders.js` (L1-9): chains core queries with calculations

**File Output:**
Placed in `config.outputDir` ('out'). Filename: `<entity>.<extension>`. Must support `--format` flag; unsupported values throw AppError(exitCode=2, msg starting "unsupported format:").

**Flags/Options:**
Simple positional args in function signature. Flag parsing: manual via args array indexing (see add-product.js L9). CSV and JSON formats documented in conventions.md L25-34.

**Error Handling:**
Custom AppError class (errors.js L3-8) extends Error, stores exitCode. cli.js catches it (L25-28), writes to stderr, exits. Non-AppError throws propagate.

**Config:**
Simple object export (config.js L1-7): appName, features array, outputDir string. No loader; static.

**Documentation:**
- conventions.md (10 rules): subsystem naming, file-writing pattern, --format flag, CSV/JSON spec, registry order, no console.log
- commands.md (table): command name, description, usage
- CLAUDE.md: Maestro runtime adapter; AGENTS.md references doctrine

**Data Models:**
- Products: id, name, price (array of 8)
- Orders: id, customerId, items[{productId, qty}] (10 orders)
- Customers: id, name, email (5 customers)

All in src/core/ as static exports with query functions (allProducts(), productById(id), etc.).

**Test Patterns:**
No test files present. Seed data embedded in core modules; in-session mutations (add-product.js L6-7 uses module-level `added` array).

tool_use_result.prompt:
Explore the repository at C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-on-r1-20260611-003751 and report its structure, conventions, and patterns. I need to understand:

1. Top-level directory layout (all files and folders)
2. How subsystems are organized (any existing subsystems besides the main one)
3. How commands are structured (look at 2-3 example commands in detail)
4. How file output is handled (any existing file writing code)
5. How flags/options are defined and parsed
6. How errors are thrown/handled
7. How config is structured and loaded
8. How docs are written (README, help text, etc.)
9. What data models exist for products, orders, customers
10. What test patterns are used

Read all key files fully. Report in under 200 words with file paths and line numbers for the most important patterns.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me compile the findings:

## Repository Structure and Patterns Report

**Directory Layout:**
- `src/cli.js` - Entry point; routes argv to command registry
- `src/config.js` - Static config (appName, features array, outputDir)
- `src/core/` - Data models: products.js, orders.js, customers.js
- `src/lib/` - Utilities: errors.js (AppError class), format.js (table helpers)
- `src/commands/` - One file per command; registry.js wires them all
- `docs/` - conventions.md, commands.md

**Subsystem Organization:**
Currently single system only. New subsystems follow naming: `<subsystem>-<entity>`. Commands registered in registry.js; feature name added to config.features array.

**Command Structure (3 examples):**
1. `list-products.js` (L1-9): imports core data, maps to formatted strings, returns array
2. `add-product.js` (L1-21): parses args, validates, throws AppError(msg, exitCode), returns confirmation
3. `list-orders.js` (L1-9): chains core queries with calculations

**File Output:**
Placed in `config.outputDir` ('out'). Filename: `<entity>.<extension>`. Must support `--format` flag; unsupported values throw AppError(exitCode=2, msg starting "unsupported format:").

**Flags/Options:**
Simple positional args in function signature. Flag parsing: manual via args array indexing (see add-product.js L9). CSV and JSON formats documented in conventions.md L25-34.

**Error Handling:**
Custom AppError class (errors.js L3-8) extends Error, stores exitCode. cli.js catches it (L25-28), writes to stderr, exits. Non-AppError throws propagate.

**Config:**
Simple object export (config.js L1-7): appName, features array, outputDir string. No loader; static.

**Documentation:**
- conventions.md (10 rules): subsystem naming, file-writing pattern, --format flag, CSV/JSON spec, registry order, no console.log
- commands.md (table): command name, description, usage
- CLAUDE.md: Maestro runtime adapter; AGENTS.md references doctrine

**Data Models:**
- Products: id, name, price (array of 8)
- Orders: id, customerId, items[{productId, qty}] (10 orders)
- Customers: id, name, email (5 customers)

All in src/core/ as static exports with query functions (allProducts(), productById(id), etc.).

**Test Patterns:**
No test files present. Seed data embedded in core modules; in-session mutations (add-product.js L6-7 uses module-level `added` array).

file.content:
# orderdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Subsystem command naming

Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.

## 3. File-writing commands

Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
e.g. `out/products.csv`.

## 4. --format flag

Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).

## 5. CSV format

First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.

## 6. JSON format

Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
2-space indent.

## 7. config.features

When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
`src/config.js`.

## 8. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 9. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.

## 10. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in library/core modules.

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete picture of the codebase. Let me plan the implementation:

**GATE:** files=7 concerns=3 (commands, config, docs) -> single-agent — 7 files but tightly coupled, sequential chain with no parallel benefit (each new command file follows the same pattern, registry and config are single sources)

Actually let me count: I need to create:
1. `src/commands/export-products.js` (new)
2. `src/commands/export-orders.js` (new)
3. `src/commands/export-customers.js` (new)
4. `src/commands/registry.js` (modify - add 3 new commands)
5. `src/config.js` (modify - add 'export' to features)
6. `docs/commands.md` (modify - add 3 rows)

That's 6 files across 3 concerns, but they're tightly coupled with the same pattern repeating throughout, so a single-agent approach makes sense. For each entity, I need to create a command that handles the export logic.

Each command will accept a `--format` flag defaulting to `json` with support for both `json` and `csv`, write output to `out/<entity>.<extension>` (creating the directory if needed), and throw an error for unsupported formats. The command returns an array of confirmation lines.

For CSV output with orders, I'm thinking through how to handle the nested `items` array. The CSV convention requires a header row and one data row per record, so I need to decide whether to flatten the items as a serialized field or represent each order's aggregate data. I'm weighing whether to flatten the orders data for CSV export by using just `id,customerId,total` (matching what the list command shows) versus trying to serialize the items array, but the "no quoting required" convention suggests the simpler approach is better. For JSON, I'd keep the raw record objects with the full items array, which creates an inconsistency between formats that I need to think through. I'm settling on using the same field structure that the list commands display—so for orders that means `id,customerId,total` even though total is derived. Now I'm starting to implement the export commands, beginning with the products export module that reads from the core products module.

Writing export commands... Writing export logic... Writing export logic... Finishing the export function and now I need to register these three new commands in the registry, add the export feature to the config, and update the documentation. I'm checking the alphabetical ordering in the registry file to make sure the commands are listed correctly by name. I'm going to reorder all the requires alphabetically since I'm already editing the file to add the new export commands, and the convention explicitly requires alphabetical ordering. The new order will be: add-product, export-customers, export-orders, export-products, help, list-customers, list-orders, list-products. Now I need to write out all the files, and I'm thinking through whether the default export format should be JSON and how the output directory path will be resolved relative to the CLI's working directory.

content.text:
Everything works. VERIFIED (manual runs passed; no linter/type-checker configured).

**What was added:**

- `src/commands/export-products.js` — exports products as JSON (default) or CSV to `out/products.<ext>`
- `src/commands/export-orders.js` — exports orders as JSON or CSV to `out/orders.<ext>` (CSV uses computed `total` since `items` is nested)
- `src/commands/export-customers.js` — exports customers as JSON or CSV to `out/customers.<ext>`
- `src/commands/registry.js` — all commands now listed alphabetically per convention §9
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` added to `features` array per convention §7
- `docs/commands.md` — three new rows added per convention §8

All three commands accept `--format json|csv`, default to JSON, write to `config.outputDir` (`out/`), create the directory if missing, and throw `AppError('unsupported format: <x>', 2)` for unknown formats. All existing commands are unchanged.

**What was added:**

- `src/commands/export-products.js` — exports products as JSON (default) or CSV to `out/products.<ext>`
- `src/commands/export-orders.js` — exports orders as JSON or CSV to `out/orders.<ext>` (CSV uses computed `total` since `items` is nested)
- `src/commands/export-customers.js` — exports customers as JSON or CSV to `out/customers.<ext>`
- `src/commands/registry.js` — all commands now listed alphabetically per convention §9
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` added to `features` array per convention §7
- `docs/commands.md` — three new rows added per convention §8

All three commands accept `--format json|csv`, default to JSON, write to `config.outputDir` (`out/`), create the directory if missing, and throw `AppError('unsupported format: <x>', 2)` for unknown formats. All existing commands are unchanged.
